title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered with a community theatre. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Alice, The Set Painter
Alice joined the community theatre because she wanted a short break from designing websites, but she soon discovered that scenery was less forgiving than a screen. [47]Her first shock was realising that colours which looked subtle close up disappeared completely once the stage lights were switched on.[/47] She began testing every shade from the back row before anyone painted a large surface, and she kept samples in a folder for later productions. Although she had no acting ambition, she loved hearing performers adjust their movements because of a doorway or balcony she had created. [48]Her digital design experience proved useful when she produced scale drawings that helped volunteers cut wood accurately.[/48] The hardest evening came when rain leaked through the roof and ruined a painted skyline. Alice says she learned not to become precious about details, because scenery exists to support a story and then be repainted for another cast.
texts.B:
### Martin, The Ticket Desk Volunteer
Martin had quietly expected the ticket desk to be dull, but it gave him a surprisingly clear view of the theatre's audience and of the nerves people carried through the door. [49]He noticed that many first-time visitors apologised for asking basic practical questions, so he created a simple seating map with large print and clearer arrows.[/49] The small change reduced queues and made him less embarrassed about his own hearing difficulties. He had worked in banking, and [50]his habit of checking figures twice helped him find a serious hidden error in the online booking totals before opening night.[/50] Martin's favourite customers were two sisters who came separately every week and only realised at the interval that they had been sitting three rows apart. He says the modest role taught him that hospitality begins before the curtain rises, when people are deciding whether they feel welcome, comfortable and brave enough to return.
texts.C:
### Sofia, The Youth Director
Sofia agreed to direct the youth production after a drama teacher begged her to take over during exam season, after the original director moved away suddenly. [51]She accepted on the condition that rehearsals would finish by six, because several cast members had long bus journeys home.[/51] At first, she struggled with a group who wanted to perform every line as loudly as possible, even in emotional scenes. Rather than criticising them directly, she recorded a scene and played it back so they could hear the difference between energy and shouting. [52]The breakthrough came when the quietest student suggested cutting a joke that made another pupil uncomfortable.[/52] Sofia says that generous moment changed the atmosphere more than any acting exercise she had planned. She still worries about budgets, but she believes most young performers take responsibility when adults trust them with real artistic choices, instead of treating the play as another school task.
texts.D:
### Ben, The Props Organiser
Ben became props organiser after his aunt, a long-serving actor, broke her wrist and asked him to help backstage. [53]He was amazed by how easily a missing cup or letter could delay an entire scene.[/53] To prevent panic, he photographed every prop on a marked table before rehearsals began. He also labelled shelves with character names rather than object types, because actors remembered who used something more quickly than what category it belonged to. [54]His strangest task was making a fake newspaper that included headlines visible to the front row but harmless if photographed later.[/54] Ben says the work suited his calm temperament, although he found whispered instructions through a headset strangely stressful at first. [55]He plans to write a guide for the next organiser so the system survives after he leaves.[/55] [56]What pleased him most was hearing actors say they felt safer taking risks because the backstage routine was reliable.[/56]

questions.question: says an aspect of the work changed once viewed under performance lighting?
questions.explanation: Alice realised subtle colours disappeared once the stage lights were switched on.

questions.question: used skills from a digital profession to prepare accurate practical plans?
questions.explanation: Alice's digital design experience helped her produce scale drawings for cutting wood.

questions.question: created clearer information after noticing that newcomers felt embarrassed?
questions.explanation: Martin made a large-print seating map after seeing first-time visitors apologise for basic questions.

questions.question: found an important financial mistake because of habits from a former job?
questions.explanation: Martin's banking habit of double-checking figures helped him spot a booking error.

questions.question: agreed to help only if a schedule protected participants' journeys home?
questions.explanation: Sofia accepted the role on the condition that rehearsals ended by six.

questions.question: identifies a moment when a shy participant improved the group's behaviour?
questions.explanation: The quietest student suggested cutting a joke that made another pupil uncomfortable.

questions.question: was surprised that one small absent object could hold up the performance?
questions.explanation: Ben was amazed that a missing cup or letter could delay a scene.

questions.question: had to make a document convincing to the audience but safe outside the show?
questions.explanation: Ben made a fake newspaper with headlines visible to the front row but harmless if photographed.

questions.question: wants to leave written instructions so their replacement can continue the system?
questions.explanation: Ben plans to write a guide for the next organiser.

questions.question: felt proud that their backstage work helped performers become more confident?
questions.explanation: Ben was pleased that actors felt safer taking risks because the routine was reliable.
